checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-006: Is BlobSidecarsByRoot deprecated protocol fully disabled after FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS?

reachability.notes: Similar to PROP-0047 but for the by-root variant. Consistent deprecation behavior across both variants is critical.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the BlobSidecarsByRoot request handler in the P2P layer. 2. Verify epoch calculation logic: confirm current_epoch >= FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS triggers full protocol disablement. 3. During transition period (FULU_FORK_EPOCH <= current_epoch < FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS): verify only pre-Fulu blobs are served when requested by root. 4. After transition: send BlobSidecarsByRoot requests and verify the protocol returns appropriate error/rejection (not data). 5. Compare deprecation behavior with BlobSidecarsByRange to ensure consistency between both variants. 6. Test edge cases at exact epoch boundaries. 7. Verify no resource exhaustion possible via deprecated protocol requests.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-54, Trust Boundary: tb-006, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-013:INV-001. Post-transition requests are minor resource waste. Inconsistent deprecation between by-range and by-root variants could confuse implementations.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does beacon-node correctly identify pre-Fulu vs post-Fulu blobs by root during transition period?

Verify that roots for post-Fulu blobs are rejected during transition. 4. Test with malformed roots to ensure proper validation. 5. checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001/tb-002: Is max_blobs_per_block from get_blob_parameters(epoch) consistent across all client implementations?

reachability.notes: Cross-client inconsistency in epoch-dependent blob parameters is the primary consensus split vector for EIP-7892. An attacker can craft blocks at parameter-change epochs to exploit any divergence.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify all implementations of get_blob_parameters() across client codebases (Lighthouse, Prysm, Teku, Lodestar, Nimbus, etc.). 2. For each parameter-change epoch boundary defined in EIP-7892, compute max_blobs_per_block across all clients. 3. Verify identical values returned for: (a) epochs before FULU_FORK_EPOCH, (b) FULU_FORK_EPOCH exactly, (c) epochs after FULU_FORK_EPOCH. 4. Test edge cases: epoch 0, epoch at fork boundary minus 1, fork boundary, fork boundary plus 1. 5. Craft test blocks with blob counts at max_blobs_per_block boundary and verify accept/reject consensus across clients. 6. Verify fork-choice implications: does a disagreement cause permanent chain split? 7. Check for off-by-one errors in epoch boundary calculations. 8. Verify any caching of blob parameters does not cause stale values across epoch transitions.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-55, Trust Boundaries: tb-001, tb-002, Source Assumption: asm-005, Source Threat: stride-008. Primary Element: p2p:SG-007:beacon_block_kzg_validation. PoC approach: identify epoch where clients compute different max_blobs_per_block and demonstrate resulting consensus split.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does beacon-node correctly enforce max_blobs_per_block limit during block/sidecar gossip validation?

reachability.notes: Cross-client inconsistency in epoch-dependent blob parameters is the primary consensus split vector for EIP-7892. An attacker can craft blocks at parameter-change epochs to exploit any divergence.

Trace how max_blobs_per_block is fetched during validation - verify it uses get_blob_parameters(block.epoch). 3. Submit blocks via P2P gossip with exactly max_blobs_per_block blobs - verify acceptance. 4. Submit blocks with max_blobs_per_block + 1 blobs - verify rejection. 5. Test at epoch boundaries where max_blobs_per_block changes value. 6. Verify rejection occurs before expensive KZG operations (DoS prevention). 7. Check that rejection produces consistent error codes across clients. 8. Verify sidecar validation uses same epoch-based parameter lookup as block validation.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-55, Covered Nodes: actor-beacon-node. Validates that internal enforcement logic correctly uses the cross-client consistent parameters.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-010/tb-011: Is proposer lookahead (EIP-7917) deterministic and consistent across all client implementations?

reachability.notes: An attacker publishes a sidecar with a proposer_index that is correct according to one client's shuffling but incorrect according to another's. This exploits cross-client proposer assignment divergence.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify all implementations of compute_proposer_for_slot() across client codebases (Lighthouse, Prysm, Teku, Lodestar, Nimbus, etc.). 2. For identical beacon state S, compute proposer assignments for all slots in the lookahead window across each client. 3. Verify identical proposer_index values returned for every slot in the lookahead window. 4. Test edge cases: (a) first slot of epoch, (b) last slot of epoch, (c) epoch boundary transitions, (d) fork epoch boundaries (FULU_FORK_EPOCH). 5. Verify shuffling algorithm implementation consistency: same RANDAO mix, same active validator set, same epoch. 6. Test with varying validator set sizes to catch integer overflow/modulo edge cases. 7. Check for off-by-one errors in slot/epoch calculations within lookahead window. 8. Verify effective_balance weighting is applied identically across clients. 9. Test proposer computation caching: ensure stale cache does not cause divergence on state transitions. 10. Craft test sidecar with proposer_index valid for one client but not another and observe accept/reject behavior.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-56, Trust Boundaries: tb-010, tb-011, Source Assumption: asm-009, Source Threat: stride-022. Primary Element: fork:SG-002:upgrade_to_fulu. PoC approach: demonstrate two clients computing different proposers for the same slot from the same state - this causes consensus split at the first divergent slot.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does gossip validation correctly reject sidecars with incorrect proposer_index?

reachability.notes: An attacker publishes a sidecar with a proposer_index that is correct according to one client's shuffling but incorrect according to another's. This exploits cross-client proposer assignment divergence.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate gossip validation logic for beacon blocks and data sidecars. 2. Trace how proposer_index is validated - verify it calls compute_proposer_for_slot(state, sidecar.slot). 3. Submit sidecar via P2P gossip with correct proposer_index for the slot - verify acceptance. 4. Submit sidecar with incorrect proposer_index (e.g., off by 1) - verify rejection with appropriate error. 5. Test at epoch boundaries where proposer shuffling changes. 6. Verify rejection occurs early in validation pipeline (before expensive signature verification). 7. Check that error responses do not leak information about expected proposer_index. 8. Verify beacon-node and validator-set actors coordinate correctly on proposer assignment.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-56, Covered Nodes: actor-beacon-node, actor-validator-set, actor-proposer. Validates that internal enforcement of proposer checks uses the cross-client consistent lookahead computation.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the proposer lookahead window correctly handle fork transitions at FULU_FORK_EPOCH?

reachability.notes: An attacker publishes a sidecar with a proposer_index that is correct according to one client's shuffling but incorrect according to another's. This exploits cross-client proposer assignment divergence.

Verify proposer lookahead window calculations across the FULU_FORK_EPOCH boundary. 3. Test slots in lookahead window that span pre-Fulu and post-Fulu epochs. 4. Verify any new proposer selection rules introduced by EIP-7917 are applied consistently. 5. Check that fork version is correctly incorporated into any domain separations affecting proposer computation. 6. Test reorg scenarios where chain tip crosses fork boundary - verify proposer assignments recalculate correctly. 7. Verify no divergence between clients when lookahead window extends into post-fork epochs from pre-fork state.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-56, Primary Element: fork:SG-002:upgrade_to_fulu. Fork transitions are high-risk for cross-client divergence due to potential differences in when new rules apply.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Is KZG commitment verification cryptographically sound against forged data column sidecars?

reachability.notes: This is the fundamental cryptographic soundness assumption. If KZG verification accepts invalid data, the entire data availability scheme is broken. Implementation bugs in the KZG library are the primary attack vector.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify all entry points where verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch is called during sidecar validation. 2. Review the KZG library implementation for: (a) correct pairing operations, (b) proper point validation (subgroup checks), (c) correct domain separation. 3. Attempt to forge a data column sidecar where S.column data is inconsistent with S.kzg_commitments but passes verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 4. Test with: (a) zero proofs, (b) identity elements, (c) points not on curve, (d) points in wrong subgroup. 5. Verify batch verification does not introduce vulnerabilities not present in individual verification. 6. Test edge cases: empty column, maximum column size, single cell. 7. Verify commitment binding: given commitment C, it should be computationally infeasible to find two different data D1 != D2 that both verify. 8. Check for timing side channels that could leak information about valid vs invalid proofs. 9. Verify KZG trusted setup is correctly loaded and validated on node startup.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-57, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Source Assumption: asm-004, Source Threat: stride-002. Primary Element: p2p:SG-003:verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs. PoC approach: forge a data column sidecar with incorrect data that passes verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch - this would demonstrate complete DA scheme compromise.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is inclusion proof verification correctly integrated with KZG commitment verification?

reachability.notes: This is the fundamental cryptographic soundness assumption. If KZG verification accepts invalid data, the entire data availability scheme is broken. Implementation bugs in the KZG library are the primary attack vector.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify how inclusion proofs link data column sidecars to beacon block roots. 2. Verify the Merkle path from sidecar commitment to block body root is validated. 3. Attempt to craft a sidecar with valid KZG proofs but invalid inclusion proof - verify rejection. 4. Attempt to craft a sidecar with valid inclusion proof but for a different commitment than the one in the sidecar. 5. Test inclusion proof verification at tree boundaries and edge cases. 6. Verify that both KZG verification AND inclusion proof verification must pass for sidecar acceptance. 7. Check ordering of verification steps - ensure inclusion proof check cannot be bypassed. 8. Verify actor-p2p-peer and actor-beacon-node correctly coordinate on both verification types.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-57, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. Both KZG soundness AND correct inclusion proof binding are required for DA security.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does KZG proof batch verification maintain soundness properties of individual verification?

reachability.notes: This is the fundamental cryptographic soundness assumption. If KZG verification accepts invalid data, the entire data availability scheme is broken. Implementation bugs in the KZG library are the primary attack vector.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Review verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch implementation for random linear combination technique. 2. Verify randomness source for batch verification is cryptographically secure. 3. Test batch of N proofs where 1 is invalid - verify batch fails. 4. Test batch of N proofs where N-1 are invalid - verify batch fails. 5. Test empty batch handling - verify defined behavior (should fail or be rejected earlier). 6. Test maximum batch size - verify no integer overflow in aggregation. 7. Verify batch verification result matches individual verification for all items in batch. 8. Check for potential rogue key attacks if proofs from multiple sources are batched. 9. Verify batch verification does not introduce false positive rate (accepting invalid proofs with non-negligible probability).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-57. Batch verification is an optimization that must not weaken security. A flaw here could allow selective invalid data injection.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Are structural validation checks performed BEFORE expensive KZG/inclusion proof verification in the gossip validation pipeline?

reachability.notes: If expensive KZG verification is performed before cheap structural checks, an attacker can trigger O(n) expensive operations with O(1) effort by sending structurally invalid sidecars.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the data_column_sidecar gossip validation function (primary element: p2p:SG-006). 2. Trace the validation order and verify the following sequence: (a) Index bounds check (column index < NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS), (b) Empty data check (sidecar.column is not empty), (c) max_blobs check (len(sidecar.kzg_commitments) <= max_blobs_per_block), (d) Length consistency check (len(column) == len(kzg_commitments) == len(kzg_proofs)), (e) THEN proposer signature verification, (f) THEN KZG proof verification, (g) THEN inclusion proof verification. 3. Craft malformed sidecars that fail each structural check and verify: (a) They are rejected immediately without triggering expensive operations, (b) Measure CPU time to confirm cheap rejection. 4. Test attack scenario: send burst of structurally invalid sidecars and measure node CPU load - should remain low. 5. Verify proposer signature check occurs before KZG verification to act as cost gate (signature verification is cheaper than KZG). 6. Check for any code paths that bypass the ordering (e.g., caching, early returns).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-58, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Source Threat: stride-003. Primary Element: p2p:SG-006:data_column_sidecar_gossip_validation. PoC approach: send structurally invalid sidecars and demonstrate disproportionate CPU cost if ordering is incorrect.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does proposer signature verification act as effective cost gate before KZG verification?

reachability.notes: If expensive KZG verification is performed before cheap structural checks, an attacker can trigger O(n) expensive operations with O(1) effort by sending structurally invalid sidecars.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Verify proposer signature check is performed after structural checks but before KZG verification. 2. Confirm that signature verification has significantly lower cost than KZG verification (benchmark both operations). 3. Test sidecars with valid structure but invalid proposer signature - verify rejection before KZG. 4. Measure the cost amplification factor: (cost of signature check) / (cost of KZG verification). 5. Verify that the proposer must be a valid validator index before signature verification (prevents invalid key lookup). 6. Check that signature verification uses correct domain separation for the fork epoch. 7. Verify no caching mechanisms allow bypassing signature check on repeated submissions.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-58, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. The signature check serves as a cost barrier - only sidecars from valid proposers trigger expensive KZG verification.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Are all structural validation checks O(1) complexity relative to sidecar size?

reachability.notes: If expensive KZG verification is performed before cheap structural checks, an attacker can trigger O(n) expensive operations with O(1) effort by sending structurally invalid sidecars.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Review each structural check for time complexity: (a) Index bounds check - verify O(1) comparison, (b) Empty check - verify O(1) length check, (c) max_blobs check - verify O(1) length comparison, (d) Length consistency - verify O(1) length comparisons (not iterating through data). 2. Verify no hidden O(n) operations in structural checks (e.g., deserialization of entire column data before length check). 3. Benchmark structural checks with maximum-size sidecars - verify constant time regardless of data size. 4. Check that SSZ deserialization does not fully parse column data before structural validation. 5. Verify memory allocation during structural checks is bounded and does not depend on attacker-controlled sizes. 6. Test with oversized fields (exceeding max values) to ensure bounds checks prevent allocation attacks.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-58. Structural checks must be truly cheap - O(n) structural checks defeat the purpose of ordering them first.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-003: Does DataColumnSidecarsByRange serve complete block responses (all columns or none) for columns in storage?

reachability.notes: Partial block responses could cause syncing nodes to conclude that some columns are unavailable when they are actually available, failing data availability checks.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the DataColumnSidecarsByRange request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-008:data_column_sidecars_by_range). 2. Identify the response construction logic that iterates over blocks and columns in the requested range. 3. For a block B with columns C1, C2, ..., Cn in storage, verify that the response either includes ALL columns for B or NONE. 4. Test scenario: request columns for a block range where server has partial column storage (e.g., columns 0-63 but not 64-127). Verify either all stored columns are returned or none are returned for that block. 5. Test with various storage states: (a) all columns available, (b) some columns available, (c) no columns available. 6. Verify no race conditions between storage reads that could cause partial responses (e.g., column pruned between iteration steps). 7. Test concurrent requests to ensure atomicity of response construction. 8. Verify response ordering is consistent and predictable. 9. Check error handling: if partial storage is detected mid-response, verify clean termination without partial block data.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-59, Trust Boundary: tb-003, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-008:INV-004. PoC approach: demonstrate a server returning partial columns for a block, causing the requester to fail data availability when columns were actually available.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the req-resp client correctly interpret partial block responses as unavailability?

reachability.notes: Partial block responses could cause syncing nodes to conclude that some columns are unavailable when they are actually available, failing data availability checks.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the client-side response parsing logic for DataColumnSidecarsByRange in actor-req-resp-client. 2. Verify that when a peer returns partial columns for a block, the client either: (a) treats the entire block as unavailable from that peer and retries with another peer, or (b) requests the missing columns specifically. 3. Test with malicious peer returning intentionally partial responses - verify the client does not conclude data is unavailable without exhausting other peers. 4. Verify timeout and retry logic handles partial responses correctly. 5. Check that partial responses do not poison caches or data structures. 6. Verify metrics/logging for partial response detection to aid debugging. 7. Test integration with data availability sampling (DAS) logic to ensure partial responses do not cause false negatives.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-59, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. The client must be resilient to malicious or buggy peers returning partial data.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is there atomicity between storage check and response generation in DataColumnSidecarsByRange?

reachability.notes: Partial block responses could cause syncing nodes to conclude that some columns are unavailable when they are actually available, failing data availability checks.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the storage layer interface used by DataColumnSidecarsByRange handler. 2. Check if there is a single atomic query to determine which columns are available for a block, or if columns are checked/fetched individually. 3. If individual checks: verify locking or snapshot isolation prevents interleaved pruning/deletion. 4. Test race condition: initiate DataColumnSidecarsByRange request while concurrently triggering column pruning. Verify no partial block responses occur. 5. Review database/storage transaction boundaries - ensure response construction happens within a consistent snapshot. 6. Verify that 'columns in storage' check and 'column fetch' are atomic or use consistent view. 7. Check for TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) vulnerabilities between availability check and data fetch. 8. Verify beacon-node correctly coordinates storage access to prevent partial responses.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-59, Covered Nodes: actor-beacon-node. Storage layer race conditions are a common source of partial/inconsistent responses.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-004: Does DataColumnSidecarsByRoot respond with at least one sidecar when matching data is available?

reachability.notes: If a server silently drops available sidecars from its response, requesting nodes would conclude the data is unavailable, failing data availability checks.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-009:data_column_sidecars_by_root). 2. Identify the response construction logic that retrieves sidecars by their root identifiers. 3. For a request containing root R where the server has a matching sidecar S in storage, verify that S is included in the response. 4. Test with single root request: request a root known to exist - verify non-empty response. 5. Test with multiple roots: request N roots where M are available - verify exactly M sidecars returned. 6. Verify no silent filtering or dropping of valid, available sidecars occurs in the response path. 7. Test edge cases: (a) request root that exists but sidecar was recently pruned, (b) request during concurrent storage operations. 8. Verify logging/metrics exist to detect silent drops for debugging purposes. 9. Check that response serialization does not truncate or drop items due to size limits without proper error signaling. 10. Test with maximum request size to ensure all matching sidecars are returned.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-60, Trust Boundary: tb-004, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-009:INV-003. PoC approach: demonstrate a server that has sidecars but returns empty response, causing the requester to incorrectly conclude data unavailability.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the req-resp client correctly handle empty responses from DataColumnSidecarsByRoot?

reachability.notes: If a server silently drops available sidecars from its response, requesting nodes would conclude the data is unavailable, failing data availability checks.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the client-side response parsing logic for DataColumnSidecarsByRoot in actor-req-resp-client. 2. Verify that when a peer returns an empty response for roots that should exist, the client retries with other peers before concluding unavailability. 3. Test with malicious peer returning empty response for valid roots - verify the client does not immediately fail DA checks. 4. Verify peer scoring: does returning empty responses for available data result in peer reputation penalty? 5. Check retry logic: how many peers are queried before concluding data is truly unavailable? 6. Verify timeout handling: distinguish between 'peer has no data' vs 'peer did not respond'. 7. Test integration with data availability sampling (DAS) to ensure empty responses from one peer do not cause false unavailability conclusions. 8. Verify beacon-node and req-resp-client coordinate correctly on retry and fallback logic.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-60, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. Client must be resilient to malicious peers returning empty responses for available data.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is there any filtering or rate-limiting logic in DataColumnSidecarsByRoot that could silently drop valid sidecars?

reachability.notes: If a server silently drops available sidecars from its response, requesting nodes would conclude the data is unavailable, failing data availability checks.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Review the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot handler for any response size limits or rate limiting that could cause partial responses. 2. Check if there are maximum response size constraints that could truncate results - if so, verify proper error signaling. 3. Verify no internal caching or filtering logic drops sidecars based on non-existence criteria. 4. Check for any priority ordering that could cause lower-priority sidecars to be dropped. 5. Review error handling paths: verify no exceptions cause partial response without error indication. 6. Test under high load: verify rate limiting does not cause silent sidecar drops. 7. Verify any request throttling returns appropriate error codes rather than silently truncating responses. 8. Check for off-by-one errors in iteration logic that could skip the last sidecar in a batch.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-60. Implementation details like size limits or rate limiting could inadvertently violate the 'respond with at least one if available' invariant.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-004: Does DataColumnSidecarsByRoot refuse to serve sidecars for blocks that fail beacon-chain state transition?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes for blocks that failed state transition. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle and distributes invalid data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-009:data_column_sidecars_by_root). 2. Identify the validation logic that checks whether a block passed beacon-chain state transition before serving its data columns. 3. Store block data for a block that fails state transition validation (e.g., invalid signature, invalid state root, double vote). 4. Request data column sidecars by root for this invalid block. 5. Verify the server returns an empty response or appropriate error, NOT the stored data columns. 6. Test various state transition failure modes: (a) invalid proposer signature, (b) invalid state root, (c) invalid parent root, (d) blocks from orphaned/pruned forks. 7. Verify the check occurs BEFORE data is serialized and sent. 8. Test race condition: store block data, then mark block as invalid - verify no window where invalid data can be served. 9. Check for caching that could serve stale positive results for blocks later marked invalid. 10. Verify logging captures attempts to request data for invalid blocks (security monitoring).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-61, Trust Boundary: tb-004, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-009:INV-005, Source Threat: stride-013. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle revealing which blocks failed state transition and distributes invalid data to the network.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is block state transition validity correctly tracked and accessible to the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot handler?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes for blocks that failed state transition. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle and distributes invalid data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify how block state transition validity is stored and tracked in the beacon node. 2. Verify the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot handler queries this validity status before serving data. 3. Check for consistency between block storage and validity tracking - no orphaned data without validity status. 4. Test scenario: block data is stored before state transition completes - verify data is not served until validation passes. 5. Verify actors actor-req-resp-client and actor-beacon-node correctly coordinate on block validity status. 6. Check that validity status is not just 'in canonical chain' but specifically 'passed state transition'. 7. Verify handling of blocks that initially passed validation but were later reorged out. 8. Test edge case: request for block root that exists in storage but has never been validated (e.g., from untrusted import).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-61, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. The validity check depends on accurate tracking of which blocks passed state transition.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Are there any bypass paths that could serve data columns for invalid blocks?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes for blocks that failed state transition. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle and distributes invalid data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Review all code paths leading to DataColumnSidecarsByRoot response generation. 2. Identify any early-return optimizations that might skip validity checks (e.g., 'block root in recent cache'). 3. Check if there are admin/debug endpoints that bypass validity checks. 4. Verify no separate 'raw data' API exists that serves data without validity checks. 5. Check for feature flags or configuration options that could disable validity checks. 6. Verify error handling paths: if validity check throws exception, verify data is not served. 7. Test with concurrent validity status updates: request data while block is being marked invalid. 8. Verify no information leakage through response timing (e.g., faster rejection for invalid blocks reveals validity status). 9. Check that migrated/imported historical data also respects validity checks.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-61. Implementation shortcuts or debug features could inadvertently bypass the validity check, serving as an oracle for block validity.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Does the gossip validation pipeline IGNORE (not REJECT) sidecars whose parent block has not yet been seen?

reachability.notes: An attacker floods sidecars referencing different unseen parents, each requiring queuing. The IGNORE queue must be bounded to prevent memory exhaustion.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the gossip validation handler for data column sidecars (primary element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_parent_seen). 2. Identify the validation step that checks if the parent block has been seen by the node. 3. Verify the validation result when parent is not seen: must return IGNORE, not REJECT. Trace the code path to confirm the return value. 4. Submit a sidecar via P2P gossip referencing a parent block that has not yet arrived - verify the sidecar is not rejected but queued/ignored. 5. Verify the sidecar is stored in a pending/orphan queue awaiting parent arrival. 6. Subsequently submit the parent block - verify the previously-ignored sidecar is re-validated and processed. 7. Test timing: verify sidecar arrives before parent, is ignored, then parent arrives, sidecar is re-validated successfully. 8. Verify IGNORE does not result in peer penalty (unlike REJECT which typically penalizes the sender). 9. Test with various parent block scenarios: (a) parent arrives within timeout, (b) parent never arrives, (c) parent arrives but is invalid.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-62, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Source Threat: stride-026. Primary Element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_parent_seen. Incorrect REJECT behavior would cause valid sidecars to be permanently dropped, breaking data availability for out-of-order arrivals.

checklist.title: Critical Boundary Check: Is the IGNORE queue for unseen-parent sidecars bounded to prevent memory exhaustion?

reachability.notes: An attacker floods sidecars referencing different unseen parents, each requiring queuing. The IGNORE queue must be bounded to prevent memory exhaustion.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the data structure used to queue sidecars awaiting parent block arrival. 2. Verify the queue has explicit size limits (maximum number of pending sidecars or maximum memory usage). 3. Determine the eviction policy when the queue is full: (a) drop oldest entries, (b) drop entries for least-seen parents, (c) reject new entries. 4. Calculate maximum memory consumption: max_queue_size * max_sidecar_size. Verify this is a reasonable bound (e.g., < 1GB). 5. Test attack scenario: flood the node with sidecars referencing unique unseen parent block roots - each requires a separate queue entry. 6. Measure memory consumption during attack - verify it is bounded and does not grow indefinitely. 7. Verify the queue bound is per-peer or global, and how it interacts with peer limits. 8. Test queue eviction: when a legitimate sidecar is evicted due to attack traffic, verify it can be re-requested or re-gossiped. 9. Verify metrics/alerts exist for queue saturation to detect ongoing attacks. 10. Check that queue operations are O(1) or O(log n) to prevent CPU exhaustion during queue management.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-62, Trust Boundary: tb-001. PoC: flood node with sidecars referencing unique unseen parents, measuring memory consumption. Unbounded queue allows trivial memory exhaustion attack.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the beacon-node correctly trigger re-validation of queued sidecars when the parent block arrives?

reachability.notes: An attacker floods sidecars referencing different unseen parents, each requiring queuing. The IGNORE queue must be bounded to prevent memory exhaustion.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the callback/hook mechanism that triggers re-validation when a new block is received. 2. Verify that when a block B arrives, all sidecars queued with parent_root == B.root are retrieved and re-validated. 3. Test the re-validation path: verify full gossip validation is applied (not a partial re-check). 4. Verify re-validation respects the same validation rules as initial validation. 5. Test ordering: if multiple sidecars reference the same parent, verify all are re-validated. 6. Test cascading: if sidecar S1 references parent B1, and B1 references parent B0 (also unseen), verify correct behavior when B0 then B1 arrive. 7. Verify no duplicate processing: if sidecar was already re-validated, prevent re-processing. 8. Test timeout: sidecars in queue too long without parent arrival should be dropped (configurable timeout). 9. Verify actor-p2p-peer and actor-beacon-node coordinate correctly on block arrival notifications and queue lookups. 10. Check for race conditions: block arrives while sidecar is being added to queue - verify no missed re-validation.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-62, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. Failure to re-validate would cause valid sidecars to be permanently stuck in the queue.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Are sidecars in the IGNORE queue protected from causing processing amplification attacks?

reachability.notes: An attacker floods sidecars referencing different unseen parents, each requiring queuing. The IGNORE queue must be bounded to prevent memory exhaustion.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Analyze the cost of queueing a sidecar vs. the cost of re-validation when parent arrives. 2. Verify that expensive operations (KZG verification) are NOT performed on queue insertion - only on re-validation. 3. Test attack scenario: attacker sends N sidecars referencing parent P, then sends parent P - verify re-validation cost is bounded. 4. Verify per-parent sidecar limits: at most NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS sidecars per block should be queued. 5. Verify duplicate detection: same sidecar submitted multiple times should not create multiple queue entries. 6. Check that queue lookup by parent_root is efficient (hash-based, not linear scan). 7. Verify that attacker cannot trigger unbounded re-validation by crafting parent blocks that match many queued sidecars. 8. Test the interaction between IGNORE queue and gossip deduplication - ensure sidecars are not re-queued if already processed. 9. Verify bounded parallelism for re-validation to prevent CPU spike when many parents arrive simultaneously.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-62. Even with a bounded queue, re-validation can be exploited if an attacker can trigger expensive operations for all queued items simultaneously.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-003: Does DataColumnSidecarsByRange return ResourceUnavailable(3) for out-of-range requests without leaking storage state information?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes with out-of-range requests and analyzes response timing or content to infer storage state.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the DataColumnSidecarsByRange request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-008:data_column_sidecars_by_range). 2. Identify the logic that determines if a requested range is outside the node's serve_range. 3. For requests outside serve_range, verify the response is exactly ResourceUnavailable(3) with no additional content. 4. Test probing attack: send requests for ranges outside serve_range and verify: (a) error code is ResourceUnavailable(3), (b) response contains no hints about actual storage boundaries, (c) no data is returned even if it exists in storage. 5. Verify the error response does not differ based on whether the data actually exists in storage vs. simply being outside serve_range. 6. Test boundary probing: systematically probe around the serve_range boundary to verify consistent error responses regardless of storage state. 7. Check for timing side channels: measure response latency for out-of-range requests - should be constant regardless of whether data exists. 8. Verify no logging or metrics exposed via other channels leak information about storage state for out-of-range queries. 9. Test with various serve_range configurations to ensure consistent behavior.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-63, Trust Boundary: tb-003, Source Threat: stride-010. Primary Element: p2p:SG-008:data_column_sidecars_by_range. PoC approach: probe nodes with out-of-range requests and analyze timing/response differences to infer storage policies.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is the serve_range boundary calculation consistent and not leaking through response variations?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes with out-of-range requests and analyzes response timing or content to infer storage state.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify how serve_range is calculated and maintained in the beacon node. 2. Verify serve_range boundaries are applied consistently regardless of actual data availability. 3. Test at exact serve_range boundaries: (a) request for slot exactly at serve_range start, (b) request for slot one before serve_range start, (c) request spanning into and out of serve_range. 4. Verify that partial-in-range requests do not reveal serve_range boundaries through differential responses. 5. Check for any configuration or state that could cause serve_range to vary unexpectedly. 6. Verify actors actor-req-resp-client and actor-beacon-node use consistent serve_range calculations. 7. Test after epoch transitions: verify serve_range adjustments do not create detectable timing windows. 8. Check that serve_range is based solely on epoch/slot, not on actual storage contents.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-63, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. The serve_range must be a deterministic function of time, not a reflection of storage state.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Are timing side channels eliminated for out-of-range DataColumnSidecarsByRange requests?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes with out-of-range requests and analyzes response timing or content to infer storage state.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the code path for out-of-range request handling vs. in-range request handling. 2. Verify the out-of-range check occurs BEFORE any storage operations are performed. 3. Measure response latency for: (a) out-of-range request where data exists in storage, (b) out-of-range request where data does not exist, (c) in-range request where data exists, (d) in-range request where data does not exist. 4. Verify (a) and (b) have statistically indistinguishable timing. 5. Check for any database lookups or I/O operations that occur before the range check. 6. Verify no caching effects could cause timing differences for repeated out-of-range queries. 7. Test under load: verify timing consistency is maintained when node is processing other requests. 8. Check for any logging or telemetry that could introduce timing variations based on storage state. 9. Verify early-exit paths do not create detectable timing fingerprints.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-63. Timing attacks can reveal storage state even when error messages are identical. The range check must be performed before any storage-dependent operations.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the node's sync state remain hidden from out-of-range request probing?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes with out-of-range requests and analyzes response timing or content to infer storage state.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Configure node in various sync states: (a) fully synced, (b) syncing (behind head), (c) just started syncing, (d) backfilling historical data. 2. Send identical out-of-range requests from all sync states. 3. Verify response is identical ResourceUnavailable(3) regardless of sync state. 4. Check that sync progress does not affect serve_range interpretation for out-of-range requests. 5. Verify no sync-state-dependent metadata is included in error responses. 6. Test probing during active sync: verify responses remain consistent as sync progresses. 7. Check for any P2P protocol metadata (e.g., peer status messages) that could leak sync state information correlated with range request responses. 8. Verify that nodes behind on sync do not return different errors that could fingerprint their sync position. 9. Test with simulated sync stall: verify responses remain consistent even when sync is stuck.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-63, Covered Nodes: actor-beacon-node. Sync state information combined with storage probing could allow attackers to target nodes in vulnerable states or identify specific node implementations.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Does the gossip validation pipeline return IGNORE (not REJECT) for sidecars with slots at or before the finalized epoch's start slot?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends sidecars for already-finalized slots. These should be IGNORED (stale data). If REJECTED, the sender is penalized for sending stale data, which could be exploited to penalize honest peers during reorgs.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the gossip validation handler for data column sidecars (primary element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_finalized). 2. Identify the finalization check: verify the condition slot > compute_start_slot_at_epoch(store.finalized_checkpoint.epoch) is correctly implemented. 3. For sidecars where slot <= compute_start_slot_at_epoch(store.finalized_checkpoint.epoch), verify the validation result is IGNORE, not REJECT. Trace the code path to confirm the return value. 4. Test at exact boundary: (a) sidecar with slot == compute_start_slot_at_epoch(finalized_epoch) should return IGNORE, (b) sidecar with slot == compute_start_slot_at_epoch(finalized_epoch) + 1 should proceed to further validation. 5. Verify IGNORE does not result in peer penalty (unlike REJECT which typically penalizes the sender). 6. Test reorg scenario: finalization moves backward temporarily during reorg recovery - verify honest peers sending previously-valid sidecars are not penalized. 7. Verify the finalized_checkpoint.epoch is correctly retrieved from the fork-choice store. 8. Test concurrent finalization updates: sidecar arrives while finalized_checkpoint is being updated - verify consistent behavior. 9. Check for off-by-one errors in epoch-to-slot calculations. 10. Verify logging captures IGNORE events for finalized-slot sidecars (debugging without penalizing).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-64, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Primary Element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_finalized. PoC approach: send sidecars for finalized slots during reorg recovery and observe if honest peers are incorrectly penalized due to REJECT instead of IGNORE.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is compute_start_slot_at_epoch correctly implemented and consistent across the gossip validation pipeline?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends sidecars for already-finalized slots. These should be IGNORED (stale data). If REJECTED, the sender is penalized for sending stale data, which could be exploited to penalize honest peers during reorgs.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the compute_start_slot_at_epoch function used in the finalization check. 2. Verify the implementation: start_slot = epoch * SLOTS_PER_EPOCH. 3. Test edge cases: (a) epoch 0, (b) very large epoch values (test for integer overflow), (c) maximum epoch value before overflow. 4. Verify the same function is used consistently across all gossip validation checks that reference finalized epoch. 5. Check for any caching of computed values that could become stale. 6. Verify SLOTS_PER_EPOCH constant is correctly defined and consistent with spec. 7. Test cross-fork behavior: verify compute_start_slot_at_epoch works correctly across fork boundaries (pre-Fulu and post-Fulu). 8. Verify beacon-node and p2p-peer actors use identical epoch-to-slot calculations.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-64, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. Incorrect epoch-to-slot calculation could cause valid sidecars to be incorrectly classified as finalized or vice versa.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the distinction between IGNORE and REJECT correctly affect peer scoring for finalized-slot sidecars?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends sidecars for already-finalized slots. These should be IGNORED (stale data). If REJECTED, the sender is penalized for sending stale data, which could be exploited to penalize honest peers during reorgs.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the peer scoring mechanism in the gossip validation pipeline. 2. Verify that IGNORE validation result does NOT trigger any peer penalty. 3. Verify that REJECT validation result DOES trigger a peer penalty. 4. Test attack scenario: attacker identifies a peer serving sidecars during reorg recovery, sends requests that cause the peer to forward stale sidecars - verify no penalty if IGNORE is correctly returned. 5. Measure peer reputation before and after receiving IGNORE-classified sidecars - should remain unchanged. 6. Verify no secondary effects of IGNORE (e.g., rate limiting, temporary bans) that could affect honest peers. 7. Test repeated IGNORE events: verify no cumulative penalty for honest peers frequently sending stale data during network partitions or reorgs. 8. Check that metrics distinguish IGNORE from REJECT for monitoring purposes. 9. Verify actor-p2p-peer correctly applies the IGNORE/REJECT distinction to its peer management logic.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-64, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. The IGNORE/REJECT distinction is specifically designed to protect honest peers from penalization during transient network conditions. Incorrect implementation allows penalization attacks.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Does the gossip validation pipeline REJECT (not IGNORE) sidecars whose parent block has FAILED validation?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends sidecars for blocks known to be invalid. If these are IGNORED instead of REJECTED, they would be re-queued indefinitely, wasting resources.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the gossip validation handler for data column sidecars (primary element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_parent_valid). 2. Identify the validation step that checks if the parent block is known AND has failed validation (e.g., invalid state transition, invalid signature, invalid proposer). 3. Verify the validation result when parent is known-invalid: must return REJECT, not IGNORE. Trace the code path to confirm the return value. 4. Distinguish from 'parent not seen' case (PROP-W4B13-62) which should return IGNORE - this property specifically covers the case where parent IS known but is INVALID. 5. Submit a sidecar via P2P gossip referencing a parent block that has been received and marked as invalid - verify the sidecar is REJECTED immediately. 6. Verify REJECT results in peer penalty for the sender (appropriate response to invalid data propagation). 7. Test various parent invalidity reasons: (a) invalid proposer signature, (b) invalid state root, (c) invalid parent root, (d) failed block processing. 8. Verify no resource leak: rejected sidecars for invalid parents should NOT be queued in any pending data structure. 9. Test timing: parent block arrives and fails validation, then sidecar for that parent arrives - verify immediate rejection. 10. Verify the 'known-invalid' check is efficient and does not require re-validation of the parent block.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-65, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Primary Element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_parent_valid. PoC approach: send sidecars for known-invalid parent blocks and show they are IGNORED instead of REJECTED, causing infinite re-queuing and resource waste.

checklist.title: Critical Boundary Check: Is the invalid-parent block status correctly tracked and queryable by the gossip validation pipeline?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends sidecars for blocks known to be invalid. If these are IGNORED instead of REJECTED, they would be re-queued indefinitely, wasting resources.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the data structure that tracks blocks which have failed validation (invalid block cache/set). 2. Verify this structure is accessible from the gossip validation pipeline for parent validity checks. 3. Check the retention policy: how long are invalid block roots retained? Verify sufficient retention to cover the gossip window. 4. Test cache eviction: when an invalid block is evicted from the cache, verify sidecars for that parent are still handled correctly (should REJECT or fall back to IGNORE if parent becomes 'unseen'). 5. Verify the invalid block tracker is updated atomically with block validation failure - no race window where sidecar could be queued before parent is marked invalid. 6. Test concurrent operations: parent block validation fails while sidecar for that parent is being validated - verify correct final state. 7. Verify memory bounds on invalid block tracker to prevent memory exhaustion via invalid block spam. 8. Check that actor-p2p-peer and actor-beacon-node share consistent view of invalid block status. 9. Test restart scenario: after node restart, verify previously-invalid blocks are either re-cached or handled correctly when their sidecars arrive.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-65, Trust Boundary: tb-001. The invalid block tracker is critical infrastructure - if it fails or has gaps, invalid-parent sidecars would be IGNORED instead of REJECTED.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the gossip validation correctly distinguish between 'parent not seen' (IGNORE) vs 'parent known-invalid' (REJECT)?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends sidecars for blocks known to be invalid. If these are IGNORED instead of REJECTED, they would be re-queued indefinitely, wasting resources.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the three parent states in the validation logic: (a) parent not seen/unknown, (b) parent seen and valid, (c) parent seen and invalid. 2. Verify the validation result for each state: (a) IGNORE for unknown parent (allows re-queuing), (b) CONTINUE for valid parent (proceed with further checks), (c) REJECT for invalid parent (immediate rejection). 3. Test state transitions: parent goes from unknown -> invalid (verify queued sidecars are re-evaluated and rejected). 4. Test the boundary between 'not seen' and 'known-invalid': when does a block become 'known' and how quickly does invalid status propagate? 5. Verify no state confusion: a parent that was temporarily 'not seen' due to network delay should eventually become 'valid' (not 'invalid'). 6. Check for races: parent validation starts (block seen but not yet validated), sidecar arrives - verify correct handling. 7. Verify logging/metrics distinguish between IGNORE (awaiting parent) and REJECT (invalid parent) for debugging and monitoring. 8. Test with rapid parent status changes: parent seen -> validated -> later marked invalid due to reorg - verify sidecar handling at each stage.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-65, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. The distinction between IGNORE and REJECT for parent status is critical - confusion leads to either dropped valid data (REJECT when should IGNORE) or resource exhaustion (IGNORE when should REJECT).

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Are sidecars awaiting unknown parents correctly transitioned to REJECT when the parent is later validated as invalid?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends sidecars for blocks known to be invalid. If these are IGNORED instead of REJECTED, they would be re-queued indefinitely, wasting resources.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the mechanism that re-evaluates queued (IGNORED) sidecars when a parent block arrives. 2. Verify that when a parent block arrives AND fails validation, all queued sidecars for that parent are removed from the queue (not re-validated and accepted). 3. Test the transition path: sidecar arrives for unknown parent (IGNORED, queued) -> parent block arrives -> parent validation fails -> sidecar should be removed from queue, NOT re-queued. 4. Verify the sidecar removal is efficient: hash-based lookup, not linear scan of queue. 5. Test with multiple sidecars queued for the same parent: all should be removed when parent is marked invalid. 6. Verify no memory leak: removed sidecars should be fully deallocated. 7. Check for race conditions: parent validation failing while sidecar is being added to queue for that parent. 8. Verify metrics track the number of sidecars removed due to invalid parent discovery. 9. Test the scenario where parent block root is known (from sidecar) but block body validation fails - verify correct sidecar handling.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-65. If queued sidecars are not removed when their parent is found invalid, they would be re-validated indefinitely, consuming resources with no possible successful outcome.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-011: Does the gossip validation pipeline REJECT sidecars where proposer_index does not match the deterministic proposer lookahead (EIP-7917)?

reachability.notes: An attacker with access to a validator private key sends sidecars for slots where they are not the assigned proposer. The proposer verification must use the correct lookahead, not stale shuffling data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the gossip validation handler for data column sidecars (primary element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_expected_proposer). 2. Identify the proposer_index validation step that compares sidecar.proposer_index against compute_proposer_for_slot(state, sidecar.slot) using EIP-7917 deterministic lookahead. 3. For sidecars where proposer_index != expected_proposer_for_slot AND shuffling is available, verify the validation result is REJECT. 4. Test attack scenario: attacker with valid validator key sends sidecar with valid signature but proposer_index set to their validator index for a slot they are NOT assigned - verify REJECTION. 5. Verify the proposer lookahead computation uses the correct state and epoch (not stale shuffling data from a previous epoch). 6. Test at epoch boundaries: verify proposer assignments are correctly computed when shuffling changes at epoch transition. 7. Test with slots at the beginning, middle, and end of the lookahead window. 8. Verify the check occurs BEFORE signature verification to prevent wasted computation on invalid proposer claims. 9. Test scenario where shuffling is NOT yet available: verify appropriate handling (IGNORE or wait for shuffling). 10. Verify error responses do not leak information about expected proposer_index.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-66, Trust Boundary: tb-011, Source Assumption: asm-009, Source Threat: stride-022. PoC: send sidecar with valid signature but wrong proposer_index for the slot and show it is accepted - this would demonstrate proposer privilege escalation.

checklist.title: Critical Boundary Check: Is the deterministic proposer lookahead (EIP-7917) correctly computed and not using stale shuffling data?

reachability.notes: An attacker with access to a validator private key sends sidecars for slots where they are not the assigned proposer. The proposer verification must use the correct lookahead, not stale shuffling data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the implementation of the deterministic proposer lookahead as specified in EIP-7917. 2. Verify the lookahead uses the correct RANDAO mix and active validator set for the target epoch. 3. Test staleness attack: (a) compute expected proposer for slot S using current state, (b) advance state (new deposits, exits, RANDAO reveal), (c) re-compute expected proposer for slot S with new state, (d) if results differ, verify gossip validation uses the CORRECT (current) state, not stale data. 4. Verify any caching of proposer assignments is invalidated when state changes (deposits, exits, slashings affecting active validator set). 5. Test at epoch boundaries where RANDAO mix changes: verify proposer assignment switches to use new mix at correct slot. 6. Verify the lookahead window length matches EIP-7917 specification. 7. Test with rapid state transitions: multiple blocks in quick succession - verify proposer lookup uses most recent finalized state or appropriate state. 8. Check for race conditions: state update occurs while proposer lookup is in progress. 9. Verify actors actor-beacon-node, actor-validator-set, and actor-proposer all use consistent lookahead computation.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-66, Primary Element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_expected_proposer. Using stale shuffling data allows an attacker to exploit the window between shuffling computation and actual slot to claim proposer rights incorrectly.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is proposer signature verification correctly coupled with proposer_index validation?

reachability.notes: An attacker with access to a validator private key sends sidecars for slots where they are not the assigned proposer. The proposer verification must use the correct lookahead, not stale shuffling data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the order of validation checks: proposer_index lookup -> signature verification. 2. Verify that signature verification uses the public key for the EXPECTED proposer (from lookahead), not the claimed proposer_index in the sidecar. 3. Test scenario: attacker sets proposer_index to validator V1 (their validator) but slot S is assigned to validator V2 - verify signature check uses V2's pubkey (should fail) or proposer_index mismatch is detected first. 4. Verify no bypass where a valid signature from ANY validator is accepted regardless of proposer assignment. 5. Test the domain separation: verify signature domain includes slot and fork version, preventing cross-slot replay. 6. Verify proposer_index bounds checking: proposer_index must be < len(validators) and validator must be active. 7. Test with proposer_index pointing to a slashed or exited validator - verify rejection. 8. Verify the validation handles the case where expected proposer pubkey lookup fails gracefully. 9. Check that actor-p2p-peer correctly enforces both proposer_index AND signature validation.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-66, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node, actor-proposer. The coupling between proposer_index validation and signature verification is critical - if decoupled, an attacker with any valid validator key could claim any slot.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the validation correctly handle the case when shuffling is NOT yet available for the sidecar's slot?

reachability.notes: An attacker with access to a validator private key sends sidecars for slots where they are not the assigned proposer. The proposer verification must use the correct lookahead, not stale shuffling data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the code path when shuffling data is not available for the requested slot (e.g., slot too far in the future, node not fully synced). 2. Verify the behavior is SAFE: either (a) IGNORE and queue until shuffling is available, or (b) REJECT and require re-submission. 3. Verify the behavior is NOT to ACCEPT without proposer_index verification - this would be a critical vulnerability. 4. Test attack scenario: attacker sends sidecars for far-future slots where shuffling cannot be computed - verify they are not accepted. 5. Verify the shuffling availability check is efficient and does not allow DoS via shuffling computation requests. 6. Test during initial sync: verify sidecars are not accepted until sufficient state is available to verify proposer assignments. 7. Test at epoch transition: shuffling for next epoch becomes available - verify queued sidecars are re-validated. 8. Verify no race condition: shuffling becomes available while sidecar is being validated - ensure consistent behavior. 9. Check that the property's requirement 'If the proposer_index does not match and the shuffling is available, the sidecar must be REJECTED' implies shuffling-unavailable has defined safe behavior.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-66. The property specifies behavior when shuffling IS available. The case when shuffling is NOT available must also be handled safely to prevent bypass attacks.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-006: Is BlobSidecarsByRoot deprecated protocol serve_range correctly bounded to [max(current_epoch - MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS, DENEB_FORK_EPOCH), FULU_FORK_EPOCH)?

reachability.notes: An attacker requests blobs by root for post-Fulu blocks or very old blocks. Consistent epoch boundary handling is critical.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Locate the BlobSidecarsByRoot request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-013:blob_sidecars_by_root_deprecated). 2. Identify the serve_range calculation logic that determines which blob roots may be served during the transition period. 3. Verify the lower bound: max(current_epoch - MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS, DENEB_FORK_EPOCH) - ensure blobs from epochs before this bound are NOT served. 4. Verify the upper bound: FULU_FORK_EPOCH (exclusive) - ensure blobs from FULU_FORK_EPOCH or later are NOT served. 5. Test at boundaries: (a) request blob root from slot at exactly DENEB_FORK_EPOCH - should be served if within retention window, (b) request blob root from slot at FULU_FORK_EPOCH - should NOT be served, (c) request blob root from slot at FULU_FORK_EPOCH - 1 - should be served if within retention window. 6. Test the sliding lower bound: as current_epoch increases, the lower bound should slide forward (pruning old blobs). Verify blobs just outside the window are rejected. 7. Test edge case: current_epoch < FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS (transition period active) vs after (protocol fully deprecated). 8. Verify error responses for out-of-range requests do not leak information about actual blob storage. 9. Test with various root formats (valid roots for in-range blocks, valid roots for out-of-range blocks, invalid roots).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-67, Trust Boundary: tb-006, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-013:INV-002, Source Assumption: asm-010. During the transition period, this deprecated protocol serves blobs only from pre-Fulu epochs within the bounded range.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the serve_range lower bound correctly handle the max() between retention window and DENEB_FORK_EPOCH?

reachability.notes: An attacker requests blobs by root for post-Fulu blocks or very old blocks. Consistent epoch boundary handling is critical.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the implementation of the max() function for lower bound calculation. 2. Test scenario A: current_epoch - MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS < DENEB_FORK_EPOCH (early after Deneb fork): verify lower bound is DENEB_FORK_EPOCH (not the negative/underflow value). 3. Test scenario B: current_epoch - MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS > DENEB_FORK_EPOCH (later in network history): verify lower bound is current_epoch - MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS. 4. Verify no integer underflow when current_epoch < MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS (though unlikely in production, must be handled safely). 5. Test at the exact crossover point where max() switches from returning DENEB_FORK_EPOCH to returning the retention window value. 6. Verify both actor-req-resp-client and actor-beacon-node use identical lower bound calculations. 7. Check for off-by-one errors in the max() comparison. 8. Verify the MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS constant matches the spec value.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-67, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. Incorrect lower bound calculation could expose very old blobs (if too low) or reject valid blobs (if too high).

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is the FULU_FORK_EPOCH upper bound (exclusive) correctly enforced for BlobSidecarsByRoot requests?

Test boundary cases: (a) blob from slot at compute_start_slot_at_epoch(FULU_FORK_EPOCH) - 1 should be served (last pre-Fulu slot), (b) blob from slot at compute_start_slot_at_epoch(FULU_FORK_EPOCH) should NOT be served (first Fulu slot). 4. Verify the slot-to-epoch conversion is correct and consistent. 5. Test with blob roots that span the fork boundary (e.g., block proposed at FULU_FORK_EPOCH but blobs committed in pre-Fulu state). 6. Verify no information leakage about whether post-Fulu blobs exist in storage. 7. Check for confusion between blob slot and block slot in epoch calculations. 8. Verify error responses for post-Fulu blob requests are identical to other out-of-range responses (no fingerprinting).

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-006: Are peers protected from scoring penalties when using deprecated BlobSidecarsByRoot protocol during the transition period?

During transition period: send a valid BlobSidecarsByRoot request for a pre-Fulu blob and verify NO peer score penalty is applied. 4. Verify the response is either: (a) the requested blob sidecar if available, or (b) an empty/not-found response WITHOUT penalty. 5. Test with various request patterns: (a) valid pre-Fulu blob root, (b) unknown blob root, (c) post-Fulu blob root (should be rejected gracefully). 6. Monitor peer scoring logs/metrics to confirm no INVALID_REQUEST, RATE_LIMITED, or SPAM penalties are applied for legitimate deprecated protocol use. 7. Verify that after the transition period ends, the protocol can be fully disabled (optionally with penalties for continued use, but that's separate from transition behavior). 8. Compare peer scoring behavior with BlobSidecarsByRange deprecated protocol to ensure identical graceful degradation.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the beacon-node correctly identify deprecated protocol requests as legitimate during transition?

Test request classification for: (a) valid blob root for pre-Fulu blob - should be VALID, (b) valid blob root for post-Fulu blob - should be VALID but with empty response (not penalty-triggering), (c) malformed blob root - should be INVALID_REQUEST (this IS a legitimate penalty case). 4. Verify the distinction between 'deprecated but valid' requests and 'malformed/malicious' requests. 5. Check that the request-response client (actor-req-resp-client) does not pre-emptively penalize deprecated protocol negotiation. 6. Verify logging correctly distinguishes deprecated protocol use from malicious behavior. 7. Test that rate limiting, if applied, is lenient during transition and does not trigger scoring penalties.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is the BlobSidecarsByRoot deprecated protocol peer scoring consistent with BlobSidecarsByRange?

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does graceful degradation prevent network fragmentation during transition?

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does the beacon-node server limit DataColumnSidecarsByRange responses to count * NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS?

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can a response exceed count * NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS sidecars for a small count value?

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does actor-beacon-node reject sidecars with invalid inclusion proofs from any source?

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Is verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs called for every data column sidecar in the gossip validation pipeline?

Verify that if verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs returns false, the sidecar is REJECTED (not IGNORE, not ACCEPT). 6. Test with a tampered data column sidecar where cells are modified but commitments/proofs appear valid. 7. Verify the REJECT reason is logged appropriately for debugging and peer scoring. 8. Check for any exception handling that might swallow KZG verification failures.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does actor-p2p-peer enforce KZG proof verification before relaying data column sidecars?

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does actor-beacon-node reject sidecars with invalid KZG proofs from any source?

Verify that verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs is called for sidecars received via: (a) gossip, (b) RPC requests (DataColumnSidecarsByRoot, DataColumnSidecarsByRange), (c) any other ingestion path. 3. checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can a sidecar with tampered column data and invalid KZG proofs be accepted by the gossip validation pipeline?

EXPECTED RESULT: The gossip validation pipeline calls verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs, which returns false (cells don't match commitments), and the sidecar is REJECTED. 6. checklist.title: Verify Pre-Condition Ordering: Does KZG proof verification occur after structural and signature validation?

Map out the validation order: (a) structural validation (fields present, sizes correct), (b) signature verification, (c) KZG proof verification. 3. Test with sidecars that fail signature verification - verify KZG verification is NOT called (early rejection). 7. Verify the validation order is consistent across all code paths (gossip, RPC, etc.). 8. Check for any parallel/async validation that might execute KZG verification before earlier checks complete.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001/tb-002/tb-003: Does bytes_to_bls_field reject integers >= BLS_MODULUS?

reachability.notes: An attacker can submit blob data via type-3 transactions or send crafted cells via P2P gossip. Blob bytes are deserialized into field elements via bytes_to_bls_field in cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001). If the range check is missing, modular reduction silently corrupts data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the bytes_to_bls_field function in the KZG implementation (cell_to_coset_evals in SG-001). 2. Verify the function performs explicit range checking: value < BLS_MODULUS MUST be enforced before any field operations. 3. Test with boundary values: (a) BLS_MODULUS - 1 (should succeed), (b) BLS_MODULUS (should fail), (c) BLS_MODULUS + 1 (should fail), (d) 2^256 - 1 (should fail). 4. Verify the rejection is a hard error, not a silent modular reduction. 5. Check all code paths that call bytes_to_bls_field: transaction processing, P2P cell reception, blob reconstruction. 6. Verify the BLS_MODULUS constant is correctly defined (52435875175126190479447740508185965837690552500527637822603658699938581184513). 7. Test with crafted blob data where bytes 0-31 represent a value >= BLS_MODULUS. 8. Verify error handling propagates correctly to reject the entire blob/cell, not just the field element.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1, Trust Boundaries: tb-001, tb-002, tb-003. This is the primary boundary check. Silent modular reduction instead of rejection would allow invalid field elements to corrupt the polynomial commitment pipeline.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Is range validation enforced when actor-blob-submitter sends type-3 transactions with blob data?

reachability.notes: An attacker can submit blob data via type-3 transactions or send crafted cells via P2P gossip. Blob bytes are deserialized into field elements via bytes_to_bls_field in cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001). If the range check is missing, modular reduction silently corrupts data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the transaction validation path for type-3 (blob) transactions. 2. Trace blob bytes from transaction submission through to bytes_to_bls_field deserialization. 3. Verify the range check occurs BEFORE any polynomial operations on the field elements. 4. Test attack vector: submit a type-3 transaction where blob versioned_hash is valid but underlying bytes contain value >= BLS_MODULUS. 5. Verify the transaction is rejected at mempool insertion, not accepted and later found invalid. 6. Check for timing differences between valid/invalid field element rejection (side-channel). 7. Verify the validation layer 'Transaction validation' listed in reachability actually performs or delegates this check. 8. Test with multiple blobs in one transaction where only one contains invalid field elements - verify entire transaction is rejected.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1, Covered Node: actor-blob-submitter. Attacker entry point via type-3 transactions. The transaction validation layer must reject blobs with out-of-range field elements before they enter the mempool.

Verify the range check occurs on received cell data BEFORE any polynomial operations. 4. Test attack vector: send crafted DataColumnSidecar via gossip where cell bytes represent value >= BLS_MODULUS. 5. Verify the sidecar is rejected (REJECT gossip status), not IGNORE or ACCEPT. 6. Check both gossip and RPC paths (DataColumnSidecarsByRoot, DataColumnSidecarsByRange). 7. Verify peer scoring: sending sidecars with invalid field elements should penalize the sender. 8. Test with cells that have valid KZG proofs computed with incorrect field elements (subtle attack). 9. Verify the 'Blob size check' validation layer appropriately gates this.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1, Covered Node: actor-p2p-peer. Attacker entry point via P2P gossip. Crafted cells with out-of-range field elements must be rejected before being stored, relayed, or used in computations.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does actor-block-proposer validate field element ranges before including blobs in blocks?
