checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the block proposal path that includes blobs/data columns. 2. Verify that any blob data included in a proposed block has passed field element range validation. 3. Check if the proposer relies on mempool pre-validation or performs its own validation. 4. Test scenario: if a blob bypasses mempool validation somehow, does the proposer catch invalid field elements? 5. Verify the proposer cannot be tricked into including blobs with invalid field elements via priority fee manipulation or other incentive attacks. 6. Check that rebuilt cells (from blob data during proposal) also undergo range validation. 7. Verify no caching mechanism could return unvalidated field elements to the proposer.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1, Covered Node: actor-block-proposer. While proposers typically receive pre-validated transactions, defense-in-depth requires validation at this layer to prevent corrupted blocks from being proposed.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does actor-validator-node validate field element ranges when verifying block data availability?

reachability.notes: An attacker can submit blob data via type-3 transactions or send crafted cells via P2P gossip. Blob bytes are deserialized into field elements via bytes_to_bls_field in cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001). If the range check is missing, modular reduction silently corrupts data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the validator's data availability verification path. 2. Trace the path from block reception through cell sampling to field element deserialization. 3. Verify that bytes_to_bls_field range checks are enforced during data availability sampling verification. 4. Test: send a block with associated data columns where cells contain out-of-range field elements. 5. Verify the validator rejects the block as unavailable (not just fails silently). 6. Check the interaction with verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch: does it fail on invalid field elements or does it operate on corrupted data? 7. Verify validators do not attest to blocks with invalid field elements even if KZG proofs superficially appear valid. 8. Test the full attestation flow to ensure no path allows attestation to corrupted data.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1, Covered Node: actor-validator-node. Validators must ensure data availability verification includes field element range checks to prevent attesting to corrupted block data.

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can out-of-range blob bytes produce cells that pass verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch?

reachability.notes: An attacker can submit blob data via type-3 transactions or send crafted cells via P2P gossip. Blob bytes are deserialized into field elements via bytes_to_bls_field in cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001). If the range check is missing, modular reduction silently corrupts data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. SETUP: Prepare a valid blob with all field elements < BLS_MODULUS. Compute its KZG commitment and cells with proofs. 2. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION: (a) Take a valid blob, (b) Replace one or more 32-byte chunks with values >= BLS_MODULUS, (c) Specifically test: BLS_MODULUS, BLS_MODULUS + 1, 2^256 - 1, and values just above BLS_MODULUS. 3. INJECTION: Submit the modified blob via type-3 transaction OR send crafted cells via P2P gossip. 4. OBSERVATION: Track whether bytes_to_bls_field applies modular reduction (silent corruption) or rejects. 5. EXPECTED RESULT: The system should reject the blob/cells at entry point. bytes_to_bls_field should return an error. 6. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: (a) bytes_to_bls_field applies modular reduction silently (value % BLS_MODULUS), (b) The resulting cells pass verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch, (c) The corrupted data propagates to other nodes, (d) Validators attest to blocks with this corrupted data. 7. PoC REQUIREMENTS per bug bounty notes: Demonstrate an out-of-range blob byte sequence that produces a cell passing verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch but committing to incorrect data. 8. Document the exact byte positions and values that trigger the vulnerability. 9. Measure consensus impact: do different implementations handle this differently?
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1, Bug Bounty Notes: 'Requires PoC demonstrating that an out-of-range blob byte sequence produces a cell that passes verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch but commits to incorrect data.'. This is the primary falsification test targeting the pre-condition. Severity justification: Violation allows an attacker to inject invalid field elements into the polynomial commitment pipeline. This corrupts KZG proofs and cells, potentially causing consensus failures where valid-looking but incorrect data passes verification across the entire network.

checklist.title: Verify Implementation Consistency: Do all bytes_to_bls_field implementations use consistent range checking?

reachability.notes: An attacker can submit blob data via type-3 transactions or send crafted cells via P2P gossip. Blob bytes are deserialized into field elements via bytes_to_bls_field in cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001). If the range check is missing, modular reduction silently corrupts data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify all implementations of bytes_to_bls_field across the codebase (may be in different modules or libraries). 2. Verify each implementation checks value < BLS_MODULUS BEFORE any field operations. 3. Compare implementations: (a) Do they all use the same BLS_MODULUS constant? (b) Do they all return errors (not silent reduction)? (c) Do they all check the same byte ordering (big-endian vs little-endian)? 4. Check for alternative field element construction paths that might bypass the standard bytes_to_bls_field. 5. Verify library dependencies (e.g., BLST, Arkworks) implement range checking correctly. 6. If multiple crypto libraries are used, verify they all agree on rejection behavior. 7. Test with the same out-of-range input across all implementations - all should reject identically. 8. Document any implementation divergence as a potential consensus split vulnerability.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1. Multiple implementations of field element deserialization across the codebase or client implementations could have inconsistent range checking, leading to consensus failures.

checklist.title: Verify Downstream Protection: If range check fails, are all downstream operations aborted?

reachability.notes: An attacker can submit blob data via type-3 transactions or send crafted cells via P2P gossip. Blob bytes are deserialized into field elements via bytes_to_bls_field in cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001). If the range check is missing, modular reduction silently corrupts data.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the call graph downstream of bytes_to_bls_field: cell_to_coset_evals -> polynomial arithmetic -> KZG proof generation. 2. Verify that if bytes_to_bls_field returns an error, NO downstream operations are executed on the invalid input. 3. Check for TOCTOU issues: once field element is validated, verify it cannot be modified before use in polynomial operations. 4. Verify error handling: does an error in one field element abort processing of the entire blob/cell, or just skip that element? 5. Check for partial state corruption: if some field elements are valid and some invalid, is partial processing possible? 6. Verify the coset evaluation (cell_to_coset_evals) receives only validated field elements. 7. Trace through polynomial arithmetic and verify no operations can produce valid-looking results from corrupted inputs. 8. Check KZG proof generation: verify it fails cleanly if given corrupted field elements (not produce an invalid proof that might pass verification elsewhere).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-1, Primary Element: SG-001:cell_to_coset_evals. The pre-condition text warns: 'Accepting out-of-range values would produce invalid field elements, corrupting all downstream polynomial arithmetic, cell computation, and KZG proof generation.'

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-008: Does compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge include ALL required elements in the Fiat-Shamir hash?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. Locate compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge function (SG-006).
2. VERIFY HASH INPUT COMPLETENESS - Confirm ALL of the following are included in the hash input:
   (1) RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN - domain separator constant
   (2) FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB - blob size parameter
   (3) FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL - cell size parameter
   (4) len(commitments) - number of commitments being batched
   (5) len(cell_indices) - number of cell indices
   (6) All commitments in order
   (7) For each k: commitment_indices[k], cell_indices[k], all coset_eval bytes, proofs[k]
3. VERIFY CANONICAL ORDER - Check that elements are added to hash in deterministic, specification-defined order.
4. VERIFY NO OMISSIONS - Compare implementation against EIP-7594 specification line by line.
5. VERIFY DOMAIN SEPARATOR - Confirm RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN is unique and not reused elsewhere.
6. ATTACK SIMULATION: If ANY element is missing, attempt to craft a proof that passes verification by manipulating the unbound field.
7. Cross-reference with other client implementations to verify identical hash construction.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2, Trust Boundary: tb-008, Source Invariant: SG-006:INV-001. Violation of Fiat-Shamir completeness breaks the soundness of the entire batch verification scheme. An attacker could forge KZG proofs that pass verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch, undermining data availability guarantees network-wide.

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can a forged proof pass batch verification if RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN is omitted from Fiat-Shamir hash?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. HYPOTHESIS: If the domain separator is not bound in the Fiat-Shamir hash, the challenge r can be computed identically for different proof contexts.
2. SETUP: Create a valid batch verification scenario with N commitments and cells.
3. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION:
   a) Compute the challenge r without including RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN
   b) Find another context (e.g., different domain) that produces the same r
   c) Use proofs from the other context with the target commitments
4. INJECT: Send the crafted data column sidecars via P2P gossip.
5. OBSERVE: Does verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch accept the forged proof?
6. EXPECTED RESULT: Implementation should always include domain separator; this attack should be impossible.
7. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: A proof computed under one domain passes verification under another domain.
8. PoC REQUIREMENT: Demonstrate a forged proof passing batch verification due to missing domain binding.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2, Bug Bounty Notes: 'Requires PoC showing a forged proof that passes batch verification due to missing Fiat-Shamir binding. This is a consensus-level vulnerability.'. Domain separator omission is a classic Fiat-Shamir vulnerability.

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can proof forgery occur if len(commitments) or len(cell_indices) is omitted from Fiat-Shamir hash?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. HYPOTHESIS: If length fields are not bound, an attacker can change the number of items in the batch while keeping r unchanged.
2. SETUP: Create a valid batch with N commitments and cells.
3. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION (missing len(commitments)):
   a) Compute challenge r for a batch of size N
   b) Create a different batch of size M (M != N) that produces the same r
   c) Use proofs from the size-N batch with the size-M batch
4. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION (missing len(cell_indices)):
   a) Similar approach: vary the number of cell indices while keeping r constant
5. INJECT: Send the crafted batch via P2P.
6. OBSERVE: Does batch verification accept the mismatched batch?
7. EXPECTED RESULT: Length fields must be bound; batch size manipulation should be impossible.
8. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: Proofs for N items pass verification for M items.
9. ADDITIONAL CHECK: Verify that length encoding prevents length extension attacks (e.g., big-endian vs little-endian, fixed-width encoding).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2. Length omission in Fiat-Shamir allows batch size manipulation attacks.

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can commitment_indices[k] omission allow commitment reordering attack?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. HYPOTHESIS: If commitment_indices[k] is not bound for each k, an attacker can reorder which commitment each cell maps to.
2. SETUP: Create a batch with commitments [C1, C2] and cells claiming to be from C1 and C2 respectively.
3. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION:
   a) Compute r without binding commitment_indices
   b) Create a reordered batch where cells from C1 claim to be from C2 and vice versa
   c) The challenge r remains the same due to missing index binding
4. INJECT: Send the reordered data via P2P gossip.
5. OBSERVE: Does verification accept cells with incorrect commitment mappings?
6. EXPECTED RESULT: Each cell must be cryptographically bound to its specific commitment.
7. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: A cell from commitment C1 passes verification as belonging to C2.
8. IMPACT: An attacker could claim to have data from one blob while actually providing data from another, corrupting data availability.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2. Commitment index binding prevents cross-commitment proof substitution.

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can cell_indices[k] omission allow cell index substitution attack?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. HYPOTHESIS: If cell_indices[k] is not bound, an attacker can substitute proofs between different cell positions.
2. SETUP: Create a batch with cells at indices [0, 1, 2, ...].
3. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION:
   a) Compute r without binding cell_indices
   b) Take a valid proof for cell index 0 and claim it's for cell index 5
   c) The challenge r remains unchanged due to missing index binding
4. INJECT: Send the substituted proof via P2P.
5. OBSERVE: Does verification accept the wrong cell index claim?
6. EXPECTED RESULT: Each proof must be bound to its specific cell index.
7. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: A proof for cell index I passes verification when claimed to be for cell index J.
8. IMPACT: Data availability could be subverted - nodes might believe they have complete cell coverage when they don't.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2. Cell index binding is critical for DAS sampling security.

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can coset_eval bytes omission allow data substitution while keeping same proof?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. HYPOTHESIS: If coset_eval bytes are not bound in the Fiat-Shamir hash, an attacker can change the cell data while keeping the same challenge r and proof.
2. SETUP: Create a valid cell with coset evaluations E and proof P.
3. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION:
   a) Compute r without binding coset_eval bytes
   b) Modify the coset evaluation data (e.g., flip a byte, replace with malicious data)
   c) The challenge r is unchanged because evaluations aren't bound
   d) Use the same proof P with the modified evaluations
4. INJECT: Send data column sidecar with modified cell data but original proof via P2P.
5. OBSERVE: Does batch verification accept the modified cell data?
6. EXPECTED RESULT: Any change to coset evaluations must invalidate the proof.
7. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: Modified cell data passes verification with original proof.
8. CRITICAL IMPACT: This would completely break data availability - attackers could provide arbitrary garbage data that passes KZG verification.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2. Coset evaluation binding is the core security guarantee - cells must cryptographically commit to their data.

checklist.title: Falsification Test: Can proofs[k] omission allow proof substitution between different cells?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. HYPOTHESIS: If proofs[k] is not bound in the hash, the same challenge could verify different proofs for the same commitment.
2. SETUP: Create two valid cells (Cell_A, Proof_A) and (Cell_B, Proof_B) for the same commitment.
3. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION:
   a) Compute r without binding proofs[k]
   b) Submit Cell_A with Proof_B (or vice versa)
   c) The challenge r is unchanged because proofs aren't bound
4. INJECT: Send the mismatched cell/proof pairs via P2P.
5. OBSERVE: Does batch verification accept the wrong proof for a given cell?
6. EXPECTED RESULT: Each proof must be bound to the specific cell it was generated for.
7. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: Proof_A passes verification for Cell_B.
8. NOTE: This tests the binding in the Fiat-Shamir challenge computation, not the KZG proof verification itself.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2. Proof binding ensures the random challenge is computed over the specific proof being verified.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does P2P gossip validation invoke batch verification with all parameters correctly passed?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. Trace the P2P gossip validation path for data column sidecars (covered node: actor-p2p-peer).
2. Identify where verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch is called during gossip validation.
3. Verify ALL parameters are passed correctly:
   - Commitments are extracted and passed in order
   - Cell indices match the sidecar claims
   - Coset evaluations are passed without modification
   - Proofs are passed without modification
4. Check for any parameter truncation, reordering, or transformation before passing to batch verification.
5. Verify the commitments used for verification match those in the beacon block header.
6. Check that cell_indices match the expected custody column assignments.
7. Confirm no caching or memoization could cause stale parameters to be used.
8. Trace error handling: if batch verification fails, is the sidecar properly rejected and sender penalized?
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2, Covered Node: actor-p2p-peer. Even correct Fiat-Shamir implementation can be bypassed if caller passes incorrect parameters.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Do validators verify Fiat-Shamir completeness before attesting to blocks with data columns?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. Trace the validator attestation path for blocks with data columns (covered node: actor-validator-node).
2. Identify where KZG batch verification is invoked before attestation.
3. Verify the validator re-validates (or trusts cached validation from P2P) before attesting.
4. Check timing: is there a TOCTOU window where data columns could be modified after verification but before attestation?
5. Verify attestation logic correctly handles batch verification failure - attestation must be withheld.
6. Check for any bypass paths where attestation could proceed without full verification.
7. Verify the validator uses the same batch verification parameters as P2P gossip validation (no parameter divergence).
8. Test: inject a block with invalid Fiat-Shamir proofs and verify the validator does NOT attest.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2, Covered Node: actor-validator-node. Validators are the final defense against invalid data columns entering consensus.

checklist.title: Verify Canonical Ordering: Is the hash input order deterministic and consistent across implementations?

reachability.notes: An attacker sends crafted data column sidecars via P2P. If any verifier-relevant data is omitted from the Fiat-Shamir hash, the attacker can manipulate the omitted field while keeping the challenge r unchanged, allowing proof forgery.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. Review compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge implementation for input ordering.
2. Verify the order matches the specification EXACTLY:
   (1) RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN
   (2) FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB
   (3) FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL
   (4) len(commitments)
   (5) len(cell_indices)
   (6) All commitments (in order)
   (7) For each k: commitment_indices[k], cell_indices[k], coset_eval bytes, proofs[k]
3. Compare ordering across multiple client implementations (Prysm, Lighthouse, Teku, Lodestar, Nimbus).
4. Check for any implementation-specific sorting or reordering that could cause divergence.
5. Verify byte encoding: big-endian vs little-endian consistency for all integer fields.
6. Test: generate a challenge from the same inputs across all implementations - they must produce identical r values.
7. CONSENSUS BUG IF: Different implementations produce different challenges for the same input batch.
8. Document any ordering assumptions in the hash function (e.g., SHA256 block boundaries).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-2. Non-canonical ordering would cause consensus splits - different clients would compute different challenges and reach different verification results.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-008: Is RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN globally unique and collision-free across all protocol contexts?

reachability.notes: A cross-protocol attack: if the domain separator collides with another protocol's domain, an attacker could construct a valid proof for one context and replay it in this context.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. HYPOTHESIS: If the domain separator collides with another protocol's domain, proofs from that protocol could be replayed.
2. ENUMERATION: Catalog ALL domain separators used across the Ethereum ecosystem:
   a) Consensus layer: BLS domain separators (DOMAIN_BEACON_PROPOSER, DOMAIN_BEACON_ATTESTER, etc.)
   b) KZG contexts: RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN, any single-proof KZG domains
   c) Execution layer: Any cryptographic domain separators
   d) Related protocols: EIP-4844, EIP-7594, any blob/data availability protocols
3. COLLISION CHECK: Verify RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN does not match ANY other domain separator:
   a) Check byte-level equality
   b) Check for prefix collisions (one domain being a prefix of another)
   c) Check for truncation vulnerabilities (if implementations truncate domains)
4. ATTACK CONSTRUCTION (if collision found):
   a) Generate a valid proof in the colliding context
   b) Construct a batch verification input that uses this proof
   c) Submit via P2P with manipulated cell/commitment data
   d) Observe if the foreign proof passes batch verification
5. UNIQUENESS VERIFICATION:
   a) Review how RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN is defined (constant? derived?)
   b) Verify it includes sufficient context to prevent collision (e.g., protocol version, purpose identifier)
   c) Check if it follows a namespacing convention that guarantees uniqueness
6. REPLAY VECTOR: Even without exact collision, check if domain separator handling has parsing vulnerabilities that could lead to effective collision.
7. EXPECTED RESULT: Domain separator MUST be provably unique across all Ethereum cryptographic contexts.
8. FALSIFICATION SUCCESS (BUG FOUND) IF: A proof valid in another context is accepted by batch cell KZG verification.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-3, Trust Boundary: tb-008, Source Invariant: SG-006:INV-002. Cross-protocol proof replay is a critical cryptographic vulnerability.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does P2P gossip validation reject proofs generated with foreign domain separators?

reachability.notes: A cross-protocol attack: if the domain separator collides with another protocol's domain, an attacker could construct a valid proof for one context and replay it in this context.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. Trace the P2P gossip validation path for data column sidecars (covered node: actor-p2p-peer).
2. Identify where domain separator is applied in challenge computation.
3. ATTACK SIMULATION:
   a) Generate a valid batch proof using a DIFFERENT domain separator (e.g., single-cell KZG domain)
   b) Package this proof in a DataColumnSidecar message
   c) Submit via P2P gossip
4. OBSERVE: Does P2P validation reject the sidecar?
5. ERROR HANDLING: Verify that:
   a) The rejection occurs at verification level (cryptographic failure, not format error)
   b) Error message does not leak domain separator value (information disclosure)
   c) Sender is appropriately penalized (scored down)
6. TIMING ANALYSIS: Check if foreign proofs cause different timing than valid proofs (timing oracle).
7. RESOURCE CONSUMPTION: Verify foreign proofs do not cause excessive resource usage before rejection.
8. EXPECTED RESULT: Foreign-domain proofs MUST fail verification and be rejected promptly.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-3, Covered Node: actor-p2p-peer. P2P layer is the first defense against cross-protocol proof injection.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Do validators correctly apply domain separator before attesting to blocks with data columns?

reachability.notes: A cross-protocol attack: if the domain separator collides with another protocol's domain, an attacker could construct a valid proof for one context and replay it in this context.

checklist.test_procedure:
1. Trace the validator attestation path for blocks with data columns (covered node: actor-validator-node).
2. Identify where batch KZG verification occurs in the attestation workflow.
3. Verify the domain separator used by validators matches RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN exactly.
4. CHECK FOR DIVERGENCE:
   a) Do validators use the same verification library as P2P gossip validation?
   b) Is there any code path where a different domain separator could be used?
   c) Are there any fallback or legacy verification modes?
5. CROSS-IMPLEMENTATION: Compare domain separator handling across all client implementations.
6. CONFIGURATION: Check if domain separator can be overridden via configuration (it should NOT be configurable).
7. VERSION HANDLING: Verify domain separator is correctly applied for blocks at/after the relevant fork epoch.
8. CONSENSUS CRITICAL: A divergence in domain separator handling between clients would cause consensus splits.
9. EXPECTED RESULT: All validators MUST use identical domain separator, hardcoded (not configurable).
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-3, Covered Node: actor-validator-node. Validators are the final consensus layer; domain separator consistency is critical.

Check if structured errors (attacker-crafted invalid cells) increase success probability. 6. Verify full entropy (~255 bits) makes this attack computationally infeasible. 7. Create PoC if entropy is found to be less than 128 bits: demonstrate batch of cells where one is invalid but verification passes.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Set up test validator node with crash monitoring and memory debugging enabled. 2. Connect as P2P peer to the validator. 3. Construct batch KZG proof verification requests with various length mismatches: a) commitment_bytes[10], cell_indices[5], cells[10], proofs_bytes[10] - shorter cell_indices, b) commitment_bytes[0], cell_indices[1], cells[1], proofs_bytes[1] - empty commitments with non-empty others, c) commitment_bytes[1000], cell_indices[1], cells[1], proofs_bytes[1] - one very large array. 4. Send each crafted request via P2P gossip protocol. 5. Monitor validator node for: a) Crash/panic (vulnerability confirmed - DoS), b) Out-of-bounds memory access (potential info leak or RCE), c) Proper error response with continued operation (correctly handled). 6. If no length validation exists: determine what index is used for iteration (first array? largest array? smallest array?). 7. Document exact failure mode and crash location if crash occurs.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-9, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-validator-node. Bug bounty eligible per property: Requires PoC showing crash or incorrect verification result from mismatched input lengths.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Does verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch validate array lengths BEFORE performing any cryptographic operations or array access?

reachability.notes: Mismatched array lengths from P2P input could cause out-of-bounds access or incorrect verification. This is the first validation checkpoint.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Open verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021) source code. 2. Identify the first line of code that accesses any of the four input arrays (commitment_bytes, cell_indices, cells, proofs_bytes). 3. Verify that length validation check appears BEFORE this first access. 4. Check for implicit array access: a) Iterating with 'for (i, item) in array.enumerate()' - uses first array's length implicitly, b) Zipping arrays - what happens if lengths differ? Silent truncation? Panic? 5. If using zip: verify it's 'zip_strict' or equivalent that panics on length mismatch. 6. If using iteration with index: verify index doesn't exceed shortest array length when accessing other arrays. 7. Check for early returns or computations that might access arrays before length validation. 8. Identify cryptographic operations (KZG proof verification) and verify they only occur after length validation passes.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-9, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-002. Pre-condition: validation MUST occur before crypto operations. Violation = undefined behavior on mismatched lengths.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Is each cell_index validated to be strictly less than CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB before use in coset_for_cell or coset_shift_for_cell?

Confirm rejection behavior: out-of-range cell_index must cause immediate function return with error, not proceed to array access. 7. Via P2P, craft data column sidecar with cell_index = 0xFFFFFFFF or maximum uint value. 10. Submit each via P2P gossip and verify rejection occurs BEFORE any array access in coset_for_cell or coset_shift_for_cell.

Bug bounty eligible: Requires PoC demonstrating node crash from out-of-range cell_index in P2P data column sidecar.

Verify that the pre-condition (cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB) is explicitly enforced, not implicitly assumed.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-10, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-004. Pre-condition type property: validation MUST occur before use. The property text explicitly states 'MUST be rejected before being used'.

checklist.title: Supporting Check: Can integer overflow in FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index bypass bounds checking or cause unexpected array access?

reachability.notes: Attacker sends cell_indices >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB via P2P. coset_shift_for_cell and coset_for_cell access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index], which would be out-of-bounds.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the types of cell_index and FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL constants. 2. Calculate if FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index can overflow for any valid or invalid cell_index value. 3. Determine what happens on overflow: a) Panic (safe but DoS), b) Wrap-around to smaller value (dangerous - could access valid memory with wrong data), c) Saturation (unlikely but check). 4. If overflow is possible and wraps: a) Calculate what cell_index values would wrap to indices 0..len(roots_brp)-1, b) Such values would pass array bounds checks but access wrong data. 5. Craft test cases with cell_index values that cause integer overflow in the multiplication. 6. Send via P2P and observe behavior: does it crash, reject, or silently use wrong data? 7. If the multiplication uses checked arithmetic (checked_mul), verify overflow returns error rather than proceeding. 8. Verify bounds check (cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB) occurs BEFORE the multiplication, preventing overflow entirely.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-10, Trust Boundary: tb-002. If bounds check occurs after multiplication, attacker could cause overflow. Proper order: 1) check cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB, 2) only then compute FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index.

checklist.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Does bytes_to_kzg_commitment perform BLS12-381 G1 subgroup check during deserialization?

reachability.notes: An attacker provides commitment or proof bytes that deserialize to a point on the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. Without subgroup checks, the pairing equation could give incorrect results.

checklist.test_procedure: 1. Identify the bytes_to_kzg_commitment deserialization function in the KZG library. 2. Locate where raw bytes are converted to BLS12-381 G1 points. 3. Verify that subgroup check is performed: a) The function should call a subgroup membership test (e.g., multiply by group order and check if result is identity), b) OR the function should use a validated deserialization that includes subgroup check. 4. Generate a test point that is ON the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup (small subgroup attack point). 5. Attempt to deserialize this non-subgroup point via bytes_to_kzg_commitment. 6. Verify the function rejects the point with a clear error (not silently accepts). 7. If accepted, construct a PoC where a forged commitment passes verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 8. Check if the subgroup check uses constant-time implementation to prevent timing attacks.
checklist.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-12, Trust Boundary: tb-002, STRIDE Threat: stride-024. Missing subgroup checks in pairing-based cryptography allow forging proofs. This is a well-known attack vector (CVE-2019-11510 class).
